title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | HIGHLIGHTS OF THE BUSINESS WEEK AHEAD (Published October 2, 2000) Tuesday Get the latest information on dealing with complaints about indoor air quality. The Division of Occupational Safety and Health sponsors this free class, set for 8:30 a.m.-noon at 3301 Eagle St., Suite 303. Wednesday Travel professionals from all over the state will gather at the Carlson Center in Fairbanks Wednesday-Friday for Alaska Travel Industry Association's inaugural convention and trade show. The theme of 'Tourism: Gateway to Alaska's Future' reflects the event's focus on tourism in the 21st century. Thursday Hear about BP's exploration and growth plans for Alaska from an excellent source, F.X. O'Keefe, BP's vice president and business unit leader of exploration, at the next Resource Development Council …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | BEARDSTOWN LADIES ARE BULLISH STOCK MAVENS COMING TO SPARK LOCAL INTEREST IN INVESTMENT GROUPS By Tony Hopfinger Daily News Reporter (Published October 2, 2000) The only regret Ann Brewer has about playing the stock market is that she didn't start earlier. Carol Scott. Leslie Wiley, Virginia Wolfe, and Libby McKinney of the Alaska Gold Nuggets Investment Club discuss their stocks at a recent meeting. (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Investing changed the life of this 67-year-old member of the Beardstown Ladies -- those stock-picking sweethearts from central Illinois. Granted, not every small-time investor goes on to co-author five books, appear on Phil Donahue and find herself enmeshed in a world of controversy, the kind that sparks juicy headlines like, "Jail the Beardstown Lad…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | MEETINGS (Published October 2, 2000) MONDAY Information Systems Audit and Control Association's Anchorage chapter meets for the first time from 11:30 a.m. to 1 p.m. at Alyeska Pipeline Service Co., 1835 S. Bragaw St., in the McKinley Conference Room. During this brown-bag lunch, Ed Spencer will talk about NT security. Call Mike Carter, 787-8097 or 333-0600. WEDNESDAY Alaska Travel Industry Association conducts its inaugural convention and trade show Wednesday through Friday at the Carlson Center in Fairbanks. The theme is "Tourism: Gateway to Alaska's Future," and programs will emphasize tourism opportunities for the 21st century. Highlights include a showcase of new products, new marketing plans for Alaska, the rapidly emerging world of e-business, Web site marketing, small business…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 'IT IS TIME TO EMBRACE TRIBAL GOVERNMENTS,' KNOWLES SAYS STATE 'RECOGNIZES' ALASKA TRIBES By Don Hunter Daily News Reporter (Published September 30, 2000) With a half-circle of Native leaders looking over his shoulders, Gov. Tony Knowles signed an administrative order Friday directing state agencies and officials to "recognize and respect" the 227 federally recognized tribal governments in Alaska. Gov. Tony Knowles shakes hands with Mike Williams and other tribal representatives after signing the executive order. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) The order, signed at the end of an emotional three-hour ceremony at the Alaska Native Heritage Center, creates no new authority for tribes, which are identified and empowered by the federal government. But it revokes and replaces a 1991 ord…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | INSIDE ALASKA BUSINESS (Published October 3, 2000) Sealaska invests in casino Juneau-based Sealaska Corp. plans to finalize negotiations to invest in a gaming operation on land near Escondido, Calif., of the San Pasqual band of Indians, the company said. The plan calls for First Nation Gaming to build a 40,000-square-foot temporary gaming facility followed by a 67,000-square-foot casino resort, said Robert Loescher, Sealaska chief executive. "This is an important milestone in our initiatives to diversify and broaden our revenue sources," he said. First Nation is owned by the Tunica Biloxi Tribe of Louisiana and R&R Gaming of America, he said. Sealaska is the regional Native corporation for Southeast Alaska. Unocal completes sale Unocal Corp. on Saturday completed the sale of its Niki…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | RANKLED ON THE RIVER YUKON QUEEN TOUR BOAT POSES SAFETY HAZARD, CRITICS CHARGE By Paula Dobbyn Daily News Reporter (Published September 24, 2000) On The Yukon River -- Autumn descended on the upper Yukon this month like oil paints splattered across an artist's palette. On a crisp September weekend, tourists aboard the riverboat Yukon Queen II took in the blazing yellows, burnt ochers and pomegranate reds of dying aspen and birch leaves. The orangey hues provided stark relief against the black spruce and gray rock walls towering above the swift, silty river. Few of the passengers on this sunny afternoon had any idea of the controversy they were riding on. Powered by four 1,000-horse-power engines, the Yukon Queen II travels at 40 mph along the Yukon River. Holland American officials s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | CRAB PLAN SPURS PROTEST KNOWLES URGES STEVENS TO DELAY IFQ SCHEME A YEAR By Wesley Loy And David Whitney (Published September 23, 2000) Alaska Gov. Tony Knowles is urging Sen. Ted Stevens to block an effort by Bering Sea crab fishermen and packers to divide the fishery into lucrative private shares. The problem with the plan is that some Alaska coastal communities and fishermen might be excluded, Knowles said in a letter Thursday to Stevens, R-Alaska. Representatives for some of the crab fleet, based predominantly in Washington state and Oregon, are working furiously in Washington, D.C., to persuade Stevens and other lawmakers to pass a plan to carve up the Alaska crab fisheries into individual shares for boats, similar to what was done with Alaska halibut and black cod in the mid-19…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | BIGGER CRUISE SHIPS MAY MEAN MORE TOURISTS The Associated Press (Published September 21, 2000) Ketchikan -- Cruise ships may bring more passengers to Alaska next year as companies replace older ships with larger vessels, an industry official said. With two weeks left in the summer season, about 630,000 people visited Alaska this year by cruise ship, said John Hansen, president of the North West CruiseShip Association. Hansen told the Southeast Conference this week that most sailings were full and said he expects even more people to come next year as the major lines increase capacity by about 7 percent. The total number of ships won't change, Hansen told the gathering of local government officials from around Southeast Alaska, but bigger vessels will accommodate more people. Carnival …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published October 5, 2000) Eatery opens 3rd location Village Inn Restaurant has opened a third Anchorage location, at 4403 Spenard Road. The others are in Midtown and South Anchorage. GCI, health center align General Communication Inc. has secured a $12 million contract to provide Nome's Norton Sound Health Corp. with telecom services so that village health aides can consult more effectively with doctors. The five-year contract will improve health services in 15 communities in the Bering Strait region, said David Morris, spokesman for Anchorage-based GCI. The new equipment will provide "real-time" video so doctors in Nome and Anchorage can consult with village health aides as emergencies or the need for procedures arise. Phil Wheelehan of Norton Sound Health, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Rules aim to corral farmed salmon The Associated Press (Published October 6, 2000) Victoria, British Columbia -- Fish farmers are about to face tighter regulations designed to prevent escapes of Atlantic salmon from fish farms in the province, a move they say they welcome. Besides the new aquaculture rules, provincial Fisheries Minister Corky Evans was set to announce several pilot projects to test more environmentally sensitive fish farming techniques. Ministry officials would not comment on the measures. "All equipment, materials and structures employed at a (fish farm) must be designed, constructed, installed, inspected and maintained so as to prevent escapes," the rules state without listing any penalties. The regulations, effective Oct. 31, require all British Columbia fish farm…

title: Imaginative growers strive to be stealthy
summary: It was subterfuge worthy of a James Bond movie. Tucked under the garage of a Hillside home was a secret room reachable only by a stairway hidden under a workbench that weighed several hundred pounds. Only the maker knew what to do. He'd flip a hidden switch on the bench, and with a gentle push, the seemingly immovable bench slid forward on concealed steel rails, revealing the stairs below. Even then, the owner wasn't satisfied, adding a fake bookcase below in a last-ditch attempt to conceal the rooms he used to grow and process marijuana. The officers who showed up at his home weren't fooled, however. The case is one of hundreds that Lt. Col. Bob Kean has worked on as head of the National Guard Counterdrug Support Program for the past 11 years. Mat-Su drug officers have seen a whole range …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Propositions shift power to state lawmakers By Martha Bellisle Daily News Juneau Bureau (Published October 15, 2000) Juneau -- At least three ballot measures to be decided by Alaska voters next month are examples of a power grab by the state Legislature, argue constitutional authors and legal experts who say they threaten the balance between the three branches of government and could usurp the public's ability to hold government in check. That's not true, argue legislators who supported putting the measures on the ballot. They defend constitutional amendments put on the ballot by lawmakers in recent years as needed changes that address legitimate problems. At issue: How much should Alaska's constitution be modified? And do changes proposed by lawmakers take too much power away from c…

title: COPS CATCH BANK SUSPECTS
summary: Two gunmen botched a robbery of a South Anchorage credit union early Tuesday morning when police got on their tail and followed them on a wild, gun-shooting chase through the Dimond Boulevard and Old Seward Highway shopping district. Cops and robbers provided a dramatic live show for shoppers and tourists that ended with two men in custody. Police identified the suspects as Raymond L. Hubbard II, 21, and William E. Piers, 25. Anchorage police and FBI agents were questioning both in relation to numerous charges, including bank robbery and attempted murder. Events began just after 8 a.m. at the Credit Union 1 branch at 1310 E. Dimond Blvd. According to police, the two robbers, one dressed in black, the other in a tan jacket, laid in wait until two women showed up for work. As one of the wome…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Technology can aid mushers on trail The Associated Press TY: Wire (Published October 8, 2000) Fairbanks -- From the helicopter hovering high above to the dog mushing teams armed with miniature radios, the marriage between technology and dog mushing has been a happy union, indeed. What once wasn't even a thought now is commonplace as mushers wear Walkmans to keep up with changing weather conditions, competitors and possible pitfalls on the course. Radio personality Monte Bowen has been working the Open North American Championship Sled Dog Race the race for 15 years, said technology and equipment have improved dramatically over the years and is amazed at how many people have come to rely on it. "Technology is making it easier and faster to cover the race," Bowen said. "And it's nice to…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Ahtna gearing up for construction By Paula Dobbyn Daily News Reporter (Published October 13, 2000) Ahtna Enterprises, a subsidiary of the Alaska Native corporation Ahtna Inc., is gearing up for a big construction project after a long period of lying fallow. With a recently awarded $5.6 million federal contract, the Anchorage-based construction company is laying the groundwork for building a visitor center and eight related facilities at Wrangell-St. Elias National Park and Preserve near Copper Center, according to Ahtna executive Wayne Johnson. "This subsidiary has been somewhat mothballed, and it was just reactivated recently. We're bidding all the projects around Anchorage and elsewhere," said Johnson, who is managing the Wrangell-St. Elias project. Ahtna Enterprises helped build t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | OUTDOOR HOTLINE (Published July 16, 2000) Spy on the busy beavers Join children's book writer Doug DeVries to spy on the beavers near the Eagle River Nature Center on Friday night at 7:30 p.m. The beavers are busy preparing for winter. DeVries will lead a hike from the nature center down to the water to watch these busy, buck-toothed rodents. The walk is free. For details, call 694-2108. Hike to Dew Lake Eagle River Nature Center volunteer Will Marshall will lead a six-mile, round-trip bushwhack to Dew Lake on Saturday. The hike is scheduled to start at 10 a.m. Marshall expects to be back at the Eagle River Nature Center by 3 p.m. The trip is limited to 12. Pre-registration and a $15 deposit are required, but the latter will be refunded if you show up. Marshall said to bring lunch, w…

title: IN CHINITNA BAY, CONCERNS GROW ALONG WITH BEAR-VIEWING CROWDS
summary: Chinitna Bay -- One. Two. Three. Four. Four bears. No, wait. Five. Six. Six and two cubs. Eight. The coastal grizzlies appear silently from the misty forest. They amble through a brilliant green meadow just inland from the bay's edge. They graze like bison but every now and then stop to look around, run or roll on the ground, all the while nobly indifferent to the wet or the wind or the watchers 100 yards away. A brown bear walks along a slough that runs through a meadow off the north shore of Chinitna Bay. The area has experienced a dramatic increase in the number of tourists who come to view the bears. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) There is natural drama in this wilderness scene, but it's subtle. The bears here aren't lunging for airborne salmon as they do at Brooks Falls or McNei…

title: TROOPERS PLAN GARBAGE WATCH
summary: Within the past three weeks, nine black bears have been shot dead in the Municipality of Anchorage -- seven in seven days -- by state wildlife officials or private citizens because the bears were considered a threat to human safety. The common denominator for eight of the bears was their attraction to human garbage, said state wildlife biologist Rick Sinnott, who shot several of the bears. The ninth was a bear that persistently sought food from hikers in Chugach State Park. The number of so-called nuisance black bears killed in the Anchorage area this year, 10 since June, is not much higher than in the past few years. Still, it's high enough that state troopers in Girdwood will begin patrolling neighborhoods and businesses this week looking for improperly stored trash, said troopers Sgt. L…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | GUN BOARD PULLED WUERCH SAYS HE, CHIEF WILL REVIEW APPLICATIONS By Karen Aho Daily News Reporter (Published August 26, 2000) Mayor George Wuerch announced Friday he intends to withdraw his proposal for a citizen board to review applications for the private transfer of machine guns, silencers, short-barreled shotguns and other federally restricted weapons. The chief of police will review the applications on his own, Wuerch said. Any rejections will require the mayor's approval. "When there's good cause to say no to an applicant, then we're going to do it," Wuerch said. "But we're going to do it together." The withdrawal of Wuerch's most controversial proposal since taking office July 1 came four days before it was due for a public hearing before the Anchorage Assembly, scheduled for T…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | TEXT OF KNOWLES' LETTER TO CARTER (Published August 25, 2000) An open letter to the Honorable Jimmy Carter Former President of the United States Dear President Carter: As a former governor, you hopefully will understand my anger and disappointment about your comments Wednesday in Anchorage urging designation of national monument status for the Coastal Plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. Alaskans welcome visitors to the 49th state with warmth and hospitality. However, I feel you abused this welcome by your actions yesterday. Without any meaningful dialogue with the people of Alaska, you used our state as a media prop and platform to project your message to President Clinton. I can only guess how you would have felt as governor if a figure of national prominence had come to G…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | WOMAN CHARGED WITH MANSLAUGHTER, DWI By Karen Aho Daily News Reporter (Published July 26, 2000) An Anchorage woman was charged this week with manslaughter and driving while intoxicated in a downtown crash earlier this year that killed another woman. Kimberly Rivas is led into the courtroom Tuesday for her arraignment on manslaughter and drunken driving charges. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Kimberly S. Rivas, 30, became the fifth Anchorage driver charged within the past month with killing or seriously injuring someone while drinking and driving. Police said she ran a red light and broadsided another car at an intersection in March, killing the other driver. Her blood-alcohol level was 0.15 about 50 minutes after the crash, a charging document said. "It just amazes me why all o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | KNOWLES RIPS CARTER ON ANWR 'YOU ARE WRONG' FOR SEEKING MONUMENT, GOVERNOR SAYS By Martha Bellisle Daily News Reporter (Published August 25, 2000) In a scathing message to former President Jimmy Carter, Gov. Tony Knowles on Thursday denounced Carter's call to designate the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge coastal plain a national monument and said the ex-president had abused his welcome in Alaska. During a speech Wednesday celebrating the 20th anniversary of the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act, Carter called on President Clinton to protect the 1.5-million-acre coastal plain from oil development by creating such a monument before he leaves office. Read the full text of Gov. Tony Knowles' letter to Jimmy Carter In a hastily called news conference at his Juneau office, Kn…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | AIRLINE TO GROUND PLANES FOR INSPECTION ALASKA AIR TO CHECK PART IMPLICATED IN CRASH The Associated Press (Published August 4, 2000) Washington -- Alaska Airlines is grounding up to 18 Boeing MD-80 airliners for inspections of a part implicated in last January's crash that killed 88 people, federal regulators said Thursday. The airline reported to the Federal Aviation Administration that tools used to check jackscrews may have produced incorrect readings. The jackscrew assembly consists of a nut that rides up and down a screw as it turns to lift and lower the stabilizer. There are no indications of problems, the FAA said, but the agency is alerting other airlines using MD-80 planes - as well as DC-9 jets, which are also affected - to check their planes. Alaska Airlines Flight 261 plu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | CARTER: SAVE ANWR PLAIN EX-PRESIDENT ASKS CLINTON TO DECLARE MONUMENT STATUS By Elizabeth Manning Daily News Reporter (Published August 24, 2000) Former President Carter urged President Clinton on Wednesday to designate the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge coastal plain a national monument, which would almost certainly block oil and gas drilling there. Former President Carter says the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act has helped fuel the state's booming tourist economy by protecting its wild lands and natural beauty. (BOB HALLINEN / Anchorage Daily News) "The Arctic National Wildlife Refuge should be designated without further delay as a national monument," Carter said during a crowded luncheon celebrating the 20th anniversary of the Alaska National Interest Lands Conser…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 5 VACCINES PUT ON SCHOOL LIST EPIDEMIOLOGIST SAYS STATE FELL BEHIND IN NATIONAL RANKINGS By Ann Potempa Daily News Reporter (Published September 9, 2000) The State Board of Education and Early Development voted Friday to unanimously approve five new vaccines for children, initiating the first major overhaul of the state's immunization code in more than two decades. Before students enroll for the 2001-02 school year, they must receive vaccinations for mumps and for hepatitis A and B, both liver diseases. Anyone attending a child-care facility will need those three vaccines as well as the Hib and varicella immunizations. The Hib vaccine is used to prevent contracting Haemophilus influenzae type B, one of the leading causes of bacterial meningitis in children; the varicella vaccine is u…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | KODIAK GIRL DECLARED DEAD TWO YEARS AFTER CAMP TRIP MOTHER FILES CIVIL SUIT AGAINST MAN SHE THINKS KILLED TEEN By Sheila Toomey Daily News Reporter (Published July 7, 2000) The verdict came in at 2:15 Thursday afternoon. Jonnie Renee White is dead. "I'm so relieved," said her mother. "I feel like celebrating. It will be a great relief on the whole family." Such is the tangled emotional life forced on friends and relatives of a missing person, in this case a 16-year-old girl who vanished two years ago 40 miles south of town and has long been presumed dead by those who knew her. To Jane Speight the verdict meant two things: Now she can hold a memorial service for her dead daughter without people suggesting it's inappropriate because "you don't know for sure." "I knew from the first day…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | PROPERTY TAX CAP AT A GLANCE (Published July 29, 2000) Ballot Measure No. 4 in the November election contains two provisions that would drastically change property taxation in Alaska. IMPOSE 10-MILL (1%) PROPERTY TAX CAP * TODAY: The statewide cap is 3 percent (30 mills), though no local government sets its taxes that high. * WHAT CHANGE WOULD DO: Because boroughs and cities are both technically municipalities, each could charge up to the cap. Property in Palmer (Matanuska Susitna Borough) or Homer (Kenai Peninsula Borough), for instance, would be subject to a total of 2 percent (20 mills), while property in Anchorage and Juneau, where borough and city are unified, could be taxed only at 1 percent. * RESTRICTIONS: Unlike current state law, the initiative prevents any increase over th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | PLANE ON WAY TO LODGE CRASHES; 3 DIE 'IT WAS AMAZING WE HAD SURVIVORS THROUGH THE NIGHT, AS COLD AS IT WAS UP THERE' By Karen Aho Daily News Reporter (Published August 25, 2000) A small plane returning to a remote Alaska Peninsula fishing lodge crashed into a steep mountainside in poor weather Wednesday, killing the pilot and one passenger and leaving two other passengers, one of them mortally wounded, to survive a chilly night trapped in the dangling wreckage. The plane's position on the snow- and ice-encrusted cliff side was so delicate that when a U.S. Coast Guard helicopter hovered at the 2,700-foot site Thursday, the rotorwash began to shift the plane, Coast Guard Lt. Deborah Darminio said. Rescuers had to land at 2,000 feet, hike to the site and tie the plane down before removi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | CULTURAL COLLISION ALYESKA EXECUTIVE DISCOVERS TURBULENT TIMES AT STATE'S MOST TROUBLED COMPANY By Ben Spiess Daily News Reporter (Published August 20, 2000) The first time Melphine Evans worked for Alyeska Pipeline Service Co., in the early 1980s, a supervisor called her an "orangutan." Evans bounces along with her daughter, Jamila Reynolds, 11, while teaching the youngster about the importance of rhythm in jumping rope as they exercise at the Lake Hood Elementary School playground. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) So when Bob Malone, then-president of Alyeska, began recruiting her in 1997, Evans had one response: "Not interested." Malone was undeterred. Evans was a rising star in the Alaska business community, hailed by colleagues for her engaging personality and tough, results-o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | YOUNG BELUGA SHOT DEAD BABY FOUND BY HIKERS NEAR POINT CAMPBELL By Elizabeth Manning Daily News Reporter (Published July 26, 2000) Federal law enforcement agents Tuesday recovered the carcass of a baby beluga whale that appeared to have been shot twice in the left side. Hunting beluga whales has been illegal since May 1999, when U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens pushed a ban through Congress to protect dwindling numbers. The whale's body was found Sunday afternoon on Kincaid Beach near Point Campbell by a family on a hike. Agents learned about the dead whale Monday afternoon and retrieved it Tuesday by rolling it into a bright yellow body bag and carrying it up a dirt path. At the end of the path, they loaded the carcass into the bed of a pickup and drove slowly up the Tony Knowles Coastal Trail…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | NO STOWAWAYS FOUND ABOARD CARGO SHIP OFFICIALS CONTINUE SEARCHING CONTAINERS FOR ILLEGAL IMMIGRANTS By Liz Ruskin Daily News Reporter (Published September 7, 2000) Dutch Harbor - Federal officials said they found only boxes early this morning when they opened the shipping container that a ship's crew had suspected hid Chinese stowaways. The 860-foot container ship Manoa was diverted to this Aleutian Island port after the captain notified the U.S. Coast Guard that crew members had heard knocking coming from inside the orange container. But after unloading nearly 40 of the large metal containers to reach the suspect unit, Coast Guard spokeswoman Marsha Delaney said they found nothing but small cardboard boxes, which the manifest listed as holding human hair. "There were no humans in th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | EMPLOYER INDICTED IN 1999 CORDOVA AVALANCHE DEATH By Maureen Clark The Associated Press (Published July 26, 2000) A Bellingham, Wash., engineering company and its president were indicted on manslaughter charges Tuesday for the death of a worker killed in an avalanche near Cordova last year. The grand jury indictment, handed up in Anchorage, charged Whitewater Engineering and company president Thom Fischer each with recklessly causing Gary Stone's death. Stone, 46, was operating a backhoe in a steep canyon about seven miles northeast of Cordova when a massive load of snow slid down a 2,000-foot slope and buried him on April 15, 1999. At the time, Stone was working on a hydroelectric project being constructed by Whitewater. Neither Whitewater officials nor their lawyer, Charlie Cole of…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | VANDALS RELEASE TIGER, OTHER ANIMALS By Molly Brown Daily News Reporter (Published September 22, 2000) Early morning vandals cut the locks off several cages at the Alaska Zoo on Thursday, freeing four deer, two foxes, two lynxes, a short-eared owl and a 600-pound Siberian tiger. Tippy the fox, released early in the day, dashes across the Alaska Zoo's parking lot before ducking back through the front gate at midday. Tippy was later surrounded near some trees and caught with a net. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) The discovery set off a scramble as zoo workers went after the tiger first then managed to collect or corral nearly all the other animals. Police are trying to find whoever set the animals loose. One lynx named Chena was still missing late Thursday, said Tex Edwards, the zo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | PREPLANNING, CARE IN THE FIELD ASSURE QUALITY MOUNTS The Associated Press (Published October 1, 2000) Fairbanks -- Fairbanks taxidermist Dennis Goldbach has a simple philosophy when it comes to caring for animals in the field that you plan to have mounted. "Take care of it more than you take care of yourself and normally the hides will be in good shape," said Goldbach, a master measurer for Safari Club International and Pope & Young. Here are some taxidermy tips to remember: Talk to a taxidermist before going into the field. It's free and it can save you a bundle in the long run. The biggest mistakes hunters make in preparing animals for taxidermy are cutting capes too short and poor fleshing of hides, both of which can be addressed by your taxidermist before you head to the field. A…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | YOU'RE IN THE MONEY: $1,963 DON'T EXPECT BIG INCREASES EVERY YEAR, REVENUE CHIEF SAYS By Paul Queary The Associated Press (Published September 22, 2000) Juneau -- More than half a million Alaskans can pay for a snowmachine, a round of drinks in a really crowded bar or a few months' groceries with the largest dividend ever paid by the Alaska Permanent fund -- $1,963.86. Revenue Commissioner Wilson Condon announced the payout Thursday, giving the booming stock market of recent years credit for the growth of both the Permanent Fund and the payment the state will make to 585,800 eligible men, women and children next month. Direct deposits will be made Oct. 4, and checks will be mailed a day later. The fund grew from $14.9 billion in 1995 to nearly $28 billion this year, while the dividen…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | PRINCESS PLUGS PLAN TO CUT SHIPS' SMOKE CRUISE LINERS WILL USE CITY POWER AT DOCKSIDE By Paul Queary The Associated Press (Published September 29, 2000) Juneau -- In another bid to repair the cruise ship industry's tattered public image in Alaska, Princess Cruises announced plans Thursday to plug ships into Juneau's power supply next year as a way to lessen the smoke that gathers in the capital city's skies during the busy tourist season. The massive ships run their engines in port to generate electricity, and the exhaust from the engines sometimes collects in a thick layer under Juneau's low-hanging clouds. Princess and five other lines were cited by the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation for air quality violations this summer. Cruise ships' air and water emissions have…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | A GIANT'S FINAL DAYS? VENERABLE BEAR UNLIKELY TO SURVIVE WINTER, KATMAI RANGERS SAY By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published October 1, 2000) As the last tourists left Katmai National Park and Preserve this fall, the one-time lord of Brooks River came struggling back for what could well be his last hurrah. Some people had already given up for dead the old brown bear known as Diver, but there he was on September 18, scavenging red salmon carcasses from the river. Camp manager Mark Wagner noticed Diver's trademark Mickey Mouse ears right away but waited until he could see the old "scar saddle" on the bear's back to confirm that it was Diver. Several brown bears hunt for fish in the Brooks River near the falls in Katmai National Park. (Bob Hallinen/Anchorage Daily News) "Th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | FORT KNOX PROPOSES UNDERPASS NEAR MINE The Associated Press (Published September 27, 2000) Fairbanks -- A mining company has buckled under public pressure and wants to build a $1 million Steese Highway underpass. Fort Knox Mine had proposed allowing its ore trucks to cross the highway about every four minutes to take boulders from True North, a mine the company wants to bring into production. "It was just an argument we thought we just weren't going to win," said Bill Jeffress, Fort Knox environmental services manager. The plan calls for ore trucks to run beneath the highway. The grade of the highway would not change at the crossing point. Road construction will occur this winter if government permits are issued by the end of October or beginning of November, Jeffress said. A bypass …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | STATE SUPREME COURT BACKS 2-BALLOT PRIMARY By Sheila Toomey Daily News Reporter (Published July 26, 2000) Two justices had to listen in by phone, but Michael O'Callaghan got to tell the full Alaska Supreme Court on Tuesday why he thinks the two-ballot primary scheduled by the state for Aug. 22 should be stopped. Michael O'Callahan argued unsuccessfully Tuesday before the Supreme Court that the two-ballot primary should be stopped. Forcing voters to choose between a Republican ballot and an all-others ballot denies them the right to vote for candidates regardless of party, infringes on the secrecy of the ballot and violates Alaska law, O'Callaghan said. His arguments failed to convince the justices. Six hours later the court issued a brief order allowing the two-ballot primary to go a…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | LITTER BLOOMING IN BACKCOUNTRY By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published September 24, 2000) As a new guy on the team, Chugach State Park ranger Tom Crockett gets the dirty work: litter patrol. Just back from a wide-ranging tour of the half-million-acre wilderness reserve on the edge of Alaska's largest city, he admits to being a little amazed at what he found. It ain't just the dirty-bird anglers of Bird Creek making a mess, Crockett said last week. "The real thing that tweaked my interest was that the littering phenomenon cannot be laid at the feet of any one demographic," Crockett said. "I think the only unifying attitude of these people is that 'we can get away with this.' " Litter: Anglers discard it; Backpackers dump it; Hunters abandon it; and Even mountain runners…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | AIRLINE OFFERS SETTLEMENT CRASH LAWSUIT LAWYER DISMISSES OFFER AS 'MEDIA SHOW' By David Kravets The Associated Press (Published September 22, 2000) San Francisco -- Alaska Airlines has said for the first time that it wants to settle a lawsuit stemming from the Jan. 31 crash of Flight 261, which killed all 88 people aboard. "We would like to settle for 100 percent of the claims," the carrier's lawyer, Mark Dombroff, told a federal judge Wednesday at a court hearing filled with nearly four dozen lawyers representing the airline, victims and the jet's manufacturers. Flight 261 crashed into the Pacific Ocean off Southern California's Point Mugu en route to San Francisco from Puerto Vallarta, Mexico. Attorneys for the victims' families said it was premature to talk settlement. Under inter…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | VIDEO CLIPS SHOW ALASKA'S VIOLENT SEAS (Published September 26, 2000) Alaska's commercial fishermen know a storm doesn't have to be perfect to be perilous. Few have the opportunity -- make that the misfortune -- of seeing green-water monsters smash the bow. Swells and waves on the Gulf of Alaska and the notoriously nasty Bering Sea can make even ships seem pretty puny. Here's your chance to see it safely. The Coast Guard has a training videotape with some remarkable footage. We're posting two snippets here. Watch the video of Amber Dawn and the wave. • Modem users • High-speed users The first, titled "The Wave." It shows the 110-foot crab boat Amber Dawn plowing through horrible seas in the Gulf of Alaska, near Kodiak. It was during a 1988 crab fishery. The boat had broken down and i…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | NO SALE: MTA SHORT OF VOTERS LOW TURNOUT DOOMS PLAN FOR ACS TO PURCHASE UTILITY By Holly M. Sanders Daily News Reporter (Published September 20, 2000) Matanuska Telephone Association officials did not have to wait for the final tally Tuesday night to know that the proposed $187.5 million sale of the member-owned cooperative to Alaska Communications Systems would not pass. It didn't matter that some 64 percent of members who voted approved of the sale. The deal needed at least 16,709 members to cast a ballot and only 15,215 did. Under MTA bylaws, any sale would need a yes vote from more than half the membership, and it faced a second hurdle -- approval from at least two-thirds of all who cast ballots. Some 15,000 members mailed in their ballots; another 200 or so cast their votes duri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | YOUNG LOSES FIGHT FOR CARA By David Whitney Daily News Washington Bureau (Published October 4, 2000) Washington -- The House overwhelmingly approved a huge increase in conservation spending Tuesday after rejecting a last-minute appeal to block the measure because it did not provide cities and states the certainty of funding contained in the foundering Conservation and Reinvestment Act. The measure approved Tuesday, an $18.8 billion spending bill for the Interior Department and U.S. Forest Service, includes a new $12 billion, six-year program to direct proceeds from federal offshore drilling into parks, wildlife and coastal initiatives. It will double the $742 million going into conservation programs this year, and will top out at $2.4 billion annually in 2005 and 2006. Roughly a quar…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | PROCESSOR ACCUSED OF ILLEGAL BUY The Associated Press (Published September 22, 2000) Anchorage -- A Kenai seafood processor was indicted Wednesday by a federal grand jury on one count of conspiracy and four counts of buying illegally harvested halibut. The indictment was handed down against Kenai Custom Seafoods, owner James Hill Sr. and his son, James Hill Jr. Assistant U.S. attorney Kevin Feldis said the Hills knowingly bought halibut from a commercial fisherman who did not have an individual fishing quota required to catch halibut commercially. The alleged violations occurred between March and July of 1997. The indictment alleges that Kenai Custom Seafoods paid $42,000 for the halibut. Feldis said the company knew the fish was caught illegally, because the fisherman did not provid…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Whose feet fit these ancient footprints? Mysterious tracks offer first evidence of mammals in Alaska By Doug O'harra Daily News Reporter (Published October 5, 2000) Scientists have discovered fossilized footprints left 55 million years ago by primitive mammals and cranelike birds in an ancient forest bordering what is now the Talkeetna Mountains. Scientists don't know what animals made the footprints discovered in the Talkeetna Mountain foothills, but one likely candidate may be the coryphodon. (Drawing by University of Alaska Anchorage student Steve Trimble) The animal tracks -- including several sets found in sequence -- offer the first evidence that land mammals lived in Alaska during the period right after the extinction of the dinosaurs, according to Kevin May, University of Ala…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | LAYOFFS LOOM FOR CITY EMPLOYEES By Tim Pryor Daily News Reporter (Published September 27, 2000) Up to 153 full-time, part-time and temporary Anchorage city workers would lose their jobs under the more optimistic of Mayor George Wuerch's two spending plans for next year, the mayor's spokesman said Tuesday. All told, 289 positions, including some that are now vacant, would be cut. The cuts, about a 10 percent reduction in the city's 2,800-person work force, are included in Wuerch's "A" budget, the one he will recommend to the Anchorage Assembly if the property tax initiative doesn't pass in November. The reduction would be the first in the city's general government work force in more than 10 years. Police officers and firefighters would not be affected, but most other departments would…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | TEST CALF SHOCKINGLY SAFE FROM WOLVES By Matt Kelley The Associated Press (Published September 27, 2000) Washington -- Captive wolves in Montana are being trained to shun cattle in favor of natural prey like buffalo in an experiment in which the wolves are zapped with an electric shock when they approach the livestock. The federal agencies and private groups involved say they are trying to avoid killing the wolves, which belong to an endangered species that has been reintroduced to the wild from Canada. Critics call the experiment cruel and say that Western ranchers, not wolves, are the ones that need behavior modification. "We think it's absolutely ridiculous that we should be trying to alter the natural behavior of wild animals, particularly to benefit a private industry that uses …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | WEATHER POUNDING FARMERS HARVEST GLOOMY FOR DELTA CROPS The Associated Press (Published September 20, 2000) Fairbanks -- Delta Junction farmers, already struggling with three times the usual amount of rain, woke up to find snow covering their partially-harvested crops. Last month's rain barely allowed farmers to gather their grains, potatoes or carrots. Sunday's snow dashed hopes for a warm, dry spell to get all the crops in. "We don't know if it's doomed yet but it's pretty gloomy," said potato farmer Lyall Brasier. Brasier said he has harvested only a third of his near 90 acres of potatoes. The weather has some Delta farmers wondering how they are going to pay loans and feed livestock. Hay and straw crops, which supply Interior horse and sled dog owners, are almost nonexistent. Are…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | SLOPE PLANE CRASH KILLS 4 PIPER TOUCHED NUIQSUT RUNWAY WITH WHEELS UP; 6 HURT By Don Hunter And Peter Porco Daily News Reporters (Published September 19, 2000) Four people were killed and six injured Monday afternoon when a commuter airplane crashed at the North Slope village of Nuiqsut. Officials with the North Slope Borough Public Safety Department and the Federal Aviation Administration said the twin-engine Piper operated by Cape Smythe Air went down while trying to land at the village airstrip about 3 p.m. Nuiqsut, with about 500 residents, sits on the open Arctic tundra about 60 miles west of Prudhoe Bay and about 35 miles from the Beaufort Sea coast. The plane had stopped in Deadhorse and was to make a stop in Nuiqsut on its way to Barrow. Two who died, identified by authoritie…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | CHARGE DESCRIBES BRUTAL KILLING By Larry Campbell Daily News Reporter (Published October 2, 2000) The person who killed Della Brown and left her body in a Spenard shed beat her repeatedly with a rock and snow shovel, slit her throat, sexually assaulted her after she was dead and then showed her body to at least two of his friends, authorities said Sunday. Anchorage Police Department officers arrest Joshua Alan Wade on Saturday. Wade is a suspect in the death of Della Brown. (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) In papers charging Joshua Alan Wade, 20, with murder, prosecutors and police describe a brutal, apparently random killing after Wade and friends found Brown passed out in a street in late August. Wade later told friends in graphic detail how he killed the woman and tried to ta…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | THE SANCHEZ SOLUTION HISPANIC IMMIGRANTS TAKE TROUBLES TO RADIO JOURNALIST By Sheila Toomey Daily News Reporter (Published September 24, 2000) A hurricane wrecks Honduras. Seven thousand miles away in Alaska, Gil Sanchez's phone rings. A father goes into hiding after school officials ask for his children's immigration papers. Then he calls Gil Sanchez. Sanchez on his role of being an advocate for people: "Money and problems with the bureaucracy, language, cost, documents. I'd like to make it all better, but I cannot be a wizard for them and make it all go away. A baby dies in a traffic accident near Portage. The distraught mother wants her husband released from jail to attend the funeral. She calls Sanchez. Gilbert Sanchez is a news producer for public broadcasting, an award winning …
